ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general 'beer' class (FOODON_03301776); no specific apple-flavored beer subclass exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LQ covers beer broadly; no apple-flavored beer specific code exists. Closest parent match used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 53410008 covers beer as a substance; apple-flavored variant not separately coded. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard serving is one bottle or can of apple flavored beer, approximately 330 ml (330 g), which is the typical commercial serving size sold in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Alcoholic beverage, beer, regular, BUDWEISER (SR Legacy, FDC ID 173190)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for regular beer (FDC ID 173190), which is an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy (USDA Foundation Foods / SR Legacy). Apple flavoring adds minimal macronutrient change; sugar value is slightly elevated relative to plain beer to reflect apple flavoring contribution. The input per-100g values (energy 45.45 kcal, protein 0.45 g, carbohydrate 4.55 g, sugar 3.64 g, sodium 3.03 mg) are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy beer data and are retained as the primary per-100g values, cross-referenced and validated against USDA SR Legacy. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) are sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173190.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 66 is based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for regular beer (GI approximately 66, medium category), as published in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values, Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283. Apple-flavored beer is treated as analogous to regular beer for GI estimation purposes, as the apple flavoring does not substantially alter the fermentable carbohydrate profile. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (66) × carbohydrate per serving in grams (4.55 g per 100 g × 330 g serving = 15.015 g) / 100 = 9.9 GL units.

hypertension.key_factor: Alcohol content raises blood pressure acutely and chronically; interacts with antihypertensive medications
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid apple flavored beer entirely, as alcohol directly elevates blood pressure and antagonizes the effect of antihypertensive medications, regardless of the beverage's low sodium content (3 mg per 100 g).

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Alcohol impairs hepatic glucose regulation; residual carbohydrates and apple-derived sugars (3.64 g per 100 g) contribute to glycaemic instability
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid apple flavored beer because alcohol disrupts hepatic glucose output, can mask hypoglycaemia symptoms, and the sugar content (3.64 g per 100 g) combined with residual carbohydrates (4.55 g per 100 g) creates unpredictable glycaemic responses, particularly dangerous for those on insulin or sulfonylureas.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Alcohol consumption is associated with increased cardiovascular risk including arrhythmia, cardiomyopathy, and elevated triglycerides at regular intake levels
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid apple flavored beer, as regular alcohol consumption raises triglycerides, contributes to hypertriglyceridaemia, and increases risk of atrial fibrillation and alcoholic cardiomyopathy; the fat content is negligible (0 g per 100 g) but the alcohol-mediated cardiovascular risk is clinically significant.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Alcohol is pro-inflammatory and interacts with RA medications including methotrexate and biologics
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid apple flavored beer entirely, as alcohol is directly pro-inflammatory, can trigger disease flares, and significantly increases hepatotoxicity risk when combined with methotrexate or biologic therapies.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Apple Flavored Beer is an alcoholic malt beverage brewed from water, malted barley, hops, and yeast, with apple flavoring added to produce a fruity, refreshing taste profile. It is commercially produced and widely available in Singapore and across Southeast Asia, typically sold in 330 ml bottles or cans. The beverage has a light golden color, mild bitterness from hops, and a sweet apple aroma and finish. Per 100 g, it provides approximately 45 kcal of energy, 0.45 g protein, 0 g fat, and 4.55 g carbohydrates including 3.64 g sugars, with very low sodium at 3 mg. The alcohol content is typically 4 to 5 percent ABV. Clinically, apple flavored beer is contraindicated for individuals with hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis due to the systemic effects of alcohol on blood pressure regulation, hepatic glucose metabolism, cardiovascular function, and inflammatory pathways. It is not halal, not suitable for pregnant women, and contains gluten from malted barley. It is not a nutritionally significant food and should be consumed only by healthy adults in moderation, if at all.
common_user_queries: How many calories in apple flavored beer?
Is apple flavored beer gluten free?
Can I drink beer with high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for regular beer (FDC ID 173190), which is an approved primary source. The input GrabFood values are broadly consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and are retained as primary per-100g values. Apple flavoring introduces minor sugar variation not precisely quantifiable without brand-specific laboratory data. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for regular beer as an analogous food, as documented in the peer-reviewed Atkinson et al. (2008) international GI tables. Overall confidence is medium due to the adapted source match and apple-flavoring variability.
notes: Fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173190) for regular beer; beer is a fermented grain beverage with negligible fat by formulation — confirmed zero. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, and polyunsaturated_fat_g are all confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for regular beer. Trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. Dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for regular beer — beer undergoes filtration that removes dietary fibre. Cholesterol_mg is confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for regular beer. Cultural tags from input (Chinese cuisine, Noodles) are not applicable to this product and have been corrected to International/Western. Dietary tag 'vegetarian' from input has been removed as beer production may involve animal-derived fining agents (isinglass, gelatin); is_vegetarian and is_vegan set to false. The input ethnic_tags included 'Noodles' which is clearly erroneous for a beer product and has been discarded. Potassium, calcium, iron, B-vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, and water values are sourced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173190) for regular beer and applied as the best available approved-source approximation for apple flavored beer. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173190), an approved primary source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI source is the University of Sydney GI Database as cited in Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC (2008), Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283, an approved GI-specific source under Project Veritas v2 source policy.
